sound_type: thump on ground / squeal if caught -- alarm hind-foot thump (ground-coupled percussion warning nearby hares of predator presence) and high-pitched distress squeal (upon capture by predator), the 74th acoustic element, the first pure prey mammalian vocalization, the first ground-coupled alarm signal in the engine
ecosystem: brushy forest -- dense coniferous and mixed forest with thick understory, brushy thickets, regenerating clearcuts, shrubby riparian margins. Requires dense ground-level cover for concealment. The understory specialist. Distinct from the woodland edge of Opossum (72) and Striped Skunk (69).

coordinates.description: Reference for brushy forest habitat in western Washington Cascade foothills. The Snowshoe Hare occupies dense forest with thick understory from lowlands to subalpine zones. Found in regenerating clearcuts, young forest stands, and brushy habitat with dense ground-level cover. Thump and squeal most commonly heard in early morning or evening when hares are active and predators are hunting.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST LAGOMORPH. Thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh mammalian species. FIRST Lagomorpha. FIRST Leporidae. NEW ORDER (4th mammalian order). NEW FAMILY (33rd family). FIRST PURE PREY MAMMAL since the mammalian era began. Lagomorphs diverged from rodents approximately 80-90 million years ago. Not a rodent -- lagomorphs have 4 upper incisors (2 large + 2 peg teeth), are strictly herbivorous, practice cecotrophy, and are ecologically defined as prey.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Lagomorpha -- NEW ORDER (4th mammalian order in the engine after Rodentia, Carnivora, Didelphimorphia). Approximately 92 species in two families: Leporidae (rabbits and hares, ~63 species) and Ochotonidae (pikas, ~29 species). Diverged from Rodentia ~80-90 million years ago. Worldwide distribution except Antarctica.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Lepus from Latin lepus, 'hare.' americanus from America, named by Erxleben 1777. Common name 'Snowshoe Hare' references the large, densely furred hind feet that function as natural snowshoes on soft snow. Also called Varying Hare (seasonal coat change).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Lepus -- approximately 30 species of true hares worldwide. Distinguished from rabbits by larger size, longer ears and hind legs, precocial young (born furred, eyes open), and surface resting (no burrows). The Snowshoe Hare is the most common hare in North American forests.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh mammalian species. FOUR orders (Rodentia, Carnivora, Didelphimorphia, Lagomorpha). SEVEN families (Castoridae, Mustelidae, Mephitidae, Procyonidae, Canidae, Didelphidae, Leporidae). TWO infraclasses (Placentalia, Marsupialia). Mammalian representation rises to 17.6% (13 of 74 entries). The mammalian era extends to thirteen consecutive degrees (61-73).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Leporidae's FIRST entry at degree 73. The 33rd family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Fifth new family in five consecutive degrees (Mephitidae 69, Procyonidae 70, Canidae 71, Didelphidae 72, Leporidae 73). An unprecedented five-degree new-family streak.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Thump: low-frequency percussive impact, approximately 50-500 Hz, transmitted through ground and air. Squeal: high-pitched distress cry, approximately 2,000-8,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Thump: 100-300 Hz (the deep percussive impact). Squeal: 3,000-6,000 Hz (the piercing distress cry).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM AND DISTRESS VOCALIZATIONS. Two primary components: (1) Thump -- hind-foot percussion on the ground, an alarm signal. (2) Squeal -- high-pitched distress cry upon capture. Neither is used for social communication, territory, or mate attraction.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Thump: instantaneous percussive impact, <0.1 seconds per thump, may be repeated 2-5 times. Squeal: 1-3 seconds, high-pitched, piercing.
song_metrics.note_structure: Thump: single percussive impact, sharp onset, rapid decay. Ground-coupled -- transmitted as seismic vibration as well as airborne sound. Squeal: sustained, high-pitched, with irregular amplitude modulation (the sound of struggle).
song_metrics.note_types: Thump (alarm percussion), squeal (distress cry), soft tooth-grinding or tooth-purring (contentment, very quiet, rarely heard), maternal grunt (doe to leverets, very quiet).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Thump: single impact or rapid series (2-5 thumps in 1-2 seconds). Squeal: sustained single cry. No complex phrase structure -- these are emergency signals, not communicative sequences.
song_metrics.timbre: Thump: deep, percussive, felt as much as heard -- the sound of a dense body part striking compacted earth. Squeal: piercing, shrill, desperate -- the unmistakable sound of a prey animal in extremis.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. The thump is moderately loud (audible at 10-30 meters through air, further through ground vibration). The squeal is loud and piercing (audible at 50-100+ meters). The hare is normally silent -- the sounds are events, produced only under predation pressure.
song_metrics.tempo: Thump: rapid burst (2-5 per second when alarmed). Squeal: sustained, no rhythmic structure.
song_metrics.context: ALARM AND DISTRESS ONLY. Thump: produced when the hare detects a predator, alerting nearby hares. May be produced while the hare is still stationary in its form or during the initial moments of flight. Squeal: produced upon capture -- the sound of a predator's teeth or talons making contact. NOT produced during normal activity, foraging, or social interaction.
song_metrics.regional_variation: No documented regional variation in alarm/distress vocalizations.
song_metrics.key_property: THUMP ON GROUND / SQUEAL IF CAUGHT -- the 74th acoustic element. Alarm hind-foot percussion (ground-coupled warning signal) and high-pitched distress squeal (upon capture). E=3. The first PURE PREY MAMMALIAN vocalization. The first GROUND-COUPLED alarm. The sound of the prey species that drives the boreal food web.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized hare. 363-520 mm total length, 1.0-2.3 kg. Compact, rounded body with powerful hind legs built for explosive acceleration and direction changes. Disproportionately large hind feet (117-150 mm) that function as natural snowshoes. Seasonal coat change: brown in summer, white in winter. The forest's shape-shifter -- two colors, two seasons, one species.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 73, the diagnostic features are: (1) SNOWSHOE FEET -- disproportionately large hind feet with dense winter fur that spreads on snow for flotation. The trait that gives the species its name. (2) SEASONAL COAT CHANGE -- brown in summer, pure white in winter (except black ear tips). Second color-changing species after Short-tailed Weasel (67). (3) BLACK EAR TIPS -- retained year-round, the dark accent against both brown and white coats. (4) LARGE EYES -- set high on the head for wide-angle predator detection. (5) POWERFUL HIND LEGS -- built for explosive acceleration up to 45 km/h.
physical_description.fur_detail: SUMMER: Rich brown to grayish-brown on back and sides, white on belly. Effective camouflage against forest floor. WINTER: Pure white except for black ear tips. Effective camouflage against snow. TRANSITION: 8-10 week molt triggered by photoperiod. New white hairs grow in, replacing shed brown hairs (not a bleaching process). CLIMATE VULNERABILITY: Increasingly mismatched as climate change reduces snow duration -- white hare on brown ground is conspicuous.
physical_description.field_id: In brushy forest, regenerating clearcuts, or dense riparian margins: a medium-sized hare with disproportionately large hind feet. In winter: pure white with black ear tips -- unmistakable if seen against snow or, increasingly, conspicuous against snowless ground. In summer: brown, well-camouflaged in forest floor litter. When flushed: explosive burst from cover, zigzagging through brush at high speed, large hind feet visible in the bounding gait. Distinguished from cottontail rabbits by larger size, longer hind legs, larger feet, and seasonal coat change.

seasonal_coat_note.description: SEASONAL COAT CHANGE -- the second species in the engine to change color seasonally (after Short-tailed Weasel, SPS-067). Brown in summer, white in winter. Triggered by photoperiod (day length), not temperature or snow cover. Both retain one dark accent: hare keeps black ear tips, weasel keeps black tail tip. Both are vulnerable to climate mismatch as snow patterns change. PREDATOR-PREY PARALLEL: the weasel changes color to hunt unseen; the hare changes color to hide unseen. The same adaptation serving opposite purposes.
seasonal_coat_note.winter_color: Pure white with black ear tips
seasonal_coat_note.climate_vulnerability: Increasing mismatch as snow duration decreases -- white hare on brown ground is conspicuous and vulnerable

nodes.title: FIRST LAGOMORPH -- Order Lagomorpha Enters the Engine
nodes.description: The Snowshoe Hare introduces order Lagomorpha -- the 4th mammalian order (after Rodentia, Carnivora, Didelphimorphia). Lagomorphs are NOT rodents -- they diverged ~80-90 MYA. Strictly herbivorous, 4 upper incisors, cecotrophy. The first rabbits/hares in the engine.

nodes.title: New Family: Leporidae -- 33rd Family, Fifth New Family in Five Degrees
nodes.description: Leporidae is the 33rd family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Fifth new family in five consecutive degrees: Mephitidae (69), Procyonidae (70), Canidae (71), Didelphidae (72), Leporidae (73). An unprecedented five-degree new-family streak. The rabbit and hare family -- approximately 63 species.

nodes.title: First Pure Prey Mammal -- Every Sound Is About Predation
nodes.description: The Snowshoe Hare is the engine's first pure prey mammal. Every mammal since degree 61 has been a predator or omnivore. The hare's entire biology -- snowshoe feet, seasonal camouflage, explosive reproduction, alarm thump, distress squeal -- is shaped by the pressure of being hunted. The hare IS the food web.

nodes.title: Thump on Ground / Squeal if Caught -- 74th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Alarm and distress vocalizations: hind-foot thump (ground-coupled percussion, warning nearby hares of predators) and high-pitched distress squeal (upon capture). E=3. The first pure prey vocalization. The first ground-coupled alarm signal. The sound of the boreal food web's keystone prey.

nodes.title: Seasonal Coat Change -- Second After Short-tailed Weasel (67)
nodes.description: Brown in summer, white in winter. The second color-changing species (after SPS-067). PREDATOR-PREY PARALLEL: the weasel changes color to hunt unseen; the hare changes color to hide unseen. Both triggered by photoperiod. Both retain one dark accent (ear tips vs. tail tip). Both vulnerable to climate mismatch.

nodes.title: The Lynx-Hare Population Cycle -- Ecology's Most Famous Predator-Prey Dynamic
nodes.description: Approximately 10-year cycle of population oscillation. Documented for 200+ years through Hudson's Bay Company records. The textbook example of predator-prey dynamics. Canada Lynx specialist predator (60-97% of diet is hare). When hares crash, lynx crash 1-2 years later. The keystone prey species that drives the boreal food web.

nodes.title: Snowshoe Feet -- Natural Snowshoes for Soft Snow
nodes.description: Disproportionately large hind feet (117-150 mm) with dense winter fur that spreads on snow. The trait that gives the species its name. Provides critical advantage over predators in winter -- the hare stays on top of snow while predators sink. The engineering adaptation of a prey species.

nodes.title: Thirteenth Mammalian Entry -- The Mammalian Era at Thirteen Degrees
nodes.description: Thirteen mammalian entries across thirteen consecutive degrees (61-73). Eleven species. FOUR orders. SEVEN families. TWO infraclasses. Mammalian representation: 17.6% (13 of 74). EXTREME imbalance continues. The new order (Lagomorpha) adds genuine taxonomic breadth.

nodes.title: The LAKE Parallel
nodes.description: Both in the understory -- LAKE in Olympia's indie underground, the hare in brushy forest understory. Both quiet until startled. Both communal -- LAKE's dual vocals, the hare's predator-community dynamics. Both seasonal -- LAKE's lo-fi calendar, the hare's coat change. Both E=3.

nodes.title: Short-tailed Weasel Connection (SPS-067) -- Seasonal Color-Change Pair
nodes.description: Both change color seasonally (brown to white). Both triggered by photoperiod. Both retain one dark accent (ear tips vs. tail tip). PREDATOR-PREY PAIR: the weasel hunts unseen; the hare hides unseen. The same adaptation on opposite sides of the food web. Both vulnerable to climate mismatch.

nodes.title: Red Fox Connection (SPS-071) -- Predator-Prey
nodes.description: Foxes prey on Snowshoe Hares. The fox's pursuit versus the hare's evasion. In winter, the hare's snowshoe feet counter the fox's deeper snow penetration. Predator (71) and prey (73), two degrees apart.

nodes.title: American Marten Connection (SPS-065) -- Deep Forest Predator-Prey
nodes.description: Martens prey on hares, especially leverets. Both are deep-forest species -- the marten arboreal, the hare in the understory. Both specialists of dense forest habitat.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Connection (SPS-004) -- Forest Prey Chain
nodes.description: Both inhabit dense forest. The owl requires old-growth; the hare prefers young, dense stands. Different successional stages of the same forest type. Both affected by forest management.

cross_references.relationship: SEASONAL COLOR-CHANGE PAIR. Both brown in summer, white in winter. Both triggered by photoperiod. Both retain one dark accent (ear tips vs. tail tip). PREDATOR-PREY PAIR: the weasel changes color to hunt unseen; the hare changes color to hide unseen. Both vulnerable to climate mismatch.

PREDATOR-PREY. Foxes prey on Snowshoe Hares. The fox pursues; the hare evades. In winter, the hare's snowshoe feet counter the fox's deeper snow penetration. Two degrees apart: predator (71), prey (73).
PREDATOR-PREY IN THE DEEP FOREST. Martens prey on hares and leverets. Both are deep-forest specialists -- marten arboreal, hare in the understory. Both require dense forest habitat.

FOREST PREY CONNECTION. Both inhabit dense forest. The owl requires old-growth; the hare prefers young, dense stands with thick understory. Different successional stages. Both affected by forest management.
Thirteenth mammalian entry. Eleventh species. FIRST LAGOMORPH. Fourth order (Lagomorpha). Seventh family (Leporidae). FIRST PURE PREY MAMMAL. Mammalian representation: 17.6% (13 of 74).

Brushy forest habitat. Keystone prey species. Lynx-hare population cycle. Seasonal coat change. Snowshoe feet adaptation. The engine's first pure prey mammal adds the prey perspective to the ecological portrait.
